title: we are NOT PREPARED for the end of 2026
summary: Wes Roth discusses developments in AI toward end of 2026, covering Google's world models and agentic capabilities, OpenAI's advances in mathematical reasoning, and anticipated AI company IPOs. The commentary suggests rapid AI progress will outpace organizational and societal preparedness.
writeup: A two-host podcast discussion of the AI landscape in the wake of Google I/O, centering on Google's shift toward Gemini-everywhere integration across Search, YouTube, Gmail, and Docs, and the emerging capability to actually watch and understand video content rather than just read its metadata. The conversation surfaces a core strategic fault line: Demis Hassabis's world model thesis versus the recursive self-improvement (RSI) approach championed by Sergey Brin's revived initiative and projects like AlphaEvolve, framing Google as uniquely positioned to execute on the video-understanding frontier while the broader industry debates which AI scaling paradigm wins. A wave of AI company IPOs, including OpenAI, Anthropic, SpaceX/xAI, is treated as the economic backdrop.
key_points: Google I/O's eighth-generation TPU and YouTube's new Gemini-powered search represent a qualitative shift: rather than retrieving text about a video, the system watches videos the way a person would, picking up on presenter engagement, visual action, and personalized match signals beyond script content.

Gemini is being deployed as ambient infrastructure across Google's entire product surface, integrated into Chrome, Gmail, Docs, and YouTube, rather than as a standalone app, which the hosts argue makes this something only Google and YouTube can execute at scale.

The strategic fault line inside Google runs between Demis Hassabis's world model vision and the RSI (recursive self-improvement) approach: Sergey Brin rebooted Google's aggressive AI push toward RSI, producing AlphaEvolve and the acquisition of Sakana AI, while Hassabis holds a distinct long-term worldview that the hosts note is shared by almost no one else in the field.

The Omni video generator, paired with what the hosts call a next-generation world model underlying Google's video understanding, suggests the gap between searching for video content and generating it is narrowing inside the same infrastructure.

Anthropic is described as near-profitable and commanding a valuation high enough that its upcoming IPO, alongside OpenAI, SpaceX, and xAI, constitutes a historically unusual flurry of large AI-company public offerings.
